paragraphs.body: The Little Mermaid
Whenever Ms. Karapandža turned on the music, Haewon turned into water.

It always started with a feeling like she was swimming. Slow and measured, Haewon would raise her arms in front of her torso with just enough restraint to mimic the heavy resistance of the current. She brought her heels together into first position, then splayed all her limbs outward into second, and so forth. If she focused hard enough on dulling her surroundings, she could feel the bubbles forming on her arms, each movement causing a frenzied froth to rush to the surface. If she closed her eyes, she could forget where she was, become weightless, floating in perfect harmony to the very air around her. And just like that, she became water.

annotations.comment: I loved this opening. Really gorgeous. I feel like I’m reading your voice and POV for the first time.
annotations.context_left: ng like she was swimming. Slow and measured, Haewon would raise her arms in front of her torso with
annotations.context_right: enough restraint to mimic the heavy resistance of the current. She brought her heels together into

paragraphs.body: Even simple barre work was meaningful to her like this.

While the other girls in class took their exercises for granted, simply going through the motions like they did nearly every weekday morning, Haewon couldn’t help but treasure each movement. She just couldn’t understand why her classmates groaned whenever Ms. Karapandža clapped twice, eager to skip to the more interesting stuff. Haewon did not understand how the other girls couldn’t see the artistry in the basics. That’s why they were the basics, after all.
But Haewon never asked. Not just because most of them would shoot her dirty looks if she so much as stood too close. Rather, Haewon was not sure she would even be able to find the words for it.

annotations.comment: This is slightly perplexing because we’re close to Haewon, so we know that she does have the words for it. Maybe this is more about her feeling bold enough to ask them, or to make them understand?

paragraphs.body: “Luna, more structure to your retiré, please,” Ms. Karapandža barked as she walked, hands folded behind her back, down the line of girls stationed at the barre. Haewon continued through her movements as if she hadn’t heard anything at all. Ms. Karapandža snapped her fingers right in front of Haewon’s nose. “Gianna!”
The girl jumped back as if she had been nicked by a spark.

annotations.comment: I gave this its own line to make it clear it’s not Haewon speaking.

paragraphs.body: “Wake up! When I give a correction, I expect an answer. If not a verbal one, then certainly a visual one.”

“Yes, sorry, Ma’am,” Haewon replied, turning red. As the instructor walked on, Haewon could feel the gaze of every single girl in her class. Of course, no one would dare to directly turn their heads in front of Ms. Karapandža, but she could feel their side-eyes stabbing through her, their malicious light slicing through her body at perfect angles. If I could draw their trajectory, she thought, they’d make a perfect triangle. A beautiful retiré.

“Who does she think she is?” Their collective eyes seemed to say. “We know – she thinks she’s too good to listen to teacher. She thinks she’s better than us – but as long as we stand as a united front to her ruin, she never will be.”

annotations.comment: Strange phrasing. I wonder if this would work better if one girl stood in for all of them somehow.
annotations.context_left: ng as we stand as a

paragraphs.body: Haewon looked down in shame. She didn’t think she was better than anyone. She didn’t think her abilities so great that she could choose to ignore Ms. Karapandža’s instruction at will. Truly, truly, this was not the case at all.
She just couldn’t remember to respond to the name Luna.

annotations.comment: A devastating realization for the reader.

paragraphs.body: It was not a name she chose of her own volition. Haewon could remember, clear as anything, that first time she had stepped into the dance studio. She was of course late, since she could not find the room in such a vast building, nor could she make out the directions the desk-lady had scrawled onto note paper for her. When she finally burst through the door, the head of every young lady had turned towards her, their tightly clasped hair adding a cutting directness to the synchroneity. A boombox along the far wall twinkled with the dainty sounds of piano music, and the cascades of morning light, filtering only its purest form through the high windows, fell upon the smooth wooden floor like rectangles of impossible varnish. Haewon was sure that forever, and even beyond that, she would never forget the way it all came together; the light, the music, the girls. The way she felt, for the clearest moment, that she had stumbled upon a land of angels.

But it didn’t last long. Ms. Karapandža killed the music with a snap of a button. Haewon would immediately become familiar with her brand-new instructor’s complete intolerance for tardiness, but on that one day, she was willing to forgive Haewon based on the circumstances.

Haewon had not only arrived to class late that day – she had, in fact, arrived late to the dance school, several weeks after classes had already begun. For that briefest charmed moment, the girls swarmed around Haewon after class, eager to know her story.

annotations.comment: I was a little surprised that the instructor would allow the other girls to swarm Haewon. Maybe she introduces her first?

paragraphs.body: “You just arrived here all the way from South Korea, right?”
“Why did you start the semester so late?”
“How long have you been in New York for?”

Haewon shrunk against the wall at the barrage of questions. Her new classmates chattered endlessly; a new question would be asked before Haewon could process the last. She felt as if their words formed an ocean, and she was struggling to stand amidst wave after wave pinning her to the shore.
“What’s wrong? Don’t you speak?”

All eyes turned towards a voice from the back. The crowd parted, allowing a girl to saunter down the middle. She was tall and had an air of royalty, and Haewon suddenly felt much smaller in her presence.
“I said, don’t you speak?” The girl asked again. She stuck a hand out. “My name is Sarah. Your name was…?”

annotations.comment: Yep, here’s the girl I was looking for above.

paragraphs.body: Haewon could not say a word. She knew what the girl had asked, yet she dared not respond. She couldn’t trust whatever would come out of her mouth – the squawk of strained, broken syllables she could barely manage to clobber together, speaking a language her tongue was not well-acquainted with.
Haewon did not take Sarah’s hand. For the briefest moment, the towering girl looked taken aback. But then a cold look crossed her face, her dazzling smiling curling into something disdainful.
“Ms. K said her name was Hay-won,” a nearby girl said, wrinkling her nose.

annotations.comment: Let us know that this is a mispronunciation.
annotations.context_right: ame was Hay-won,” a nearby girl said, wrinkling her nose.

paragraphs.body: “Hay-won, is it?” Sarah asked, glancing down at the girl. “That name is just a bit too…unusual, no?” The other girls in the circle nodded fervently. “Why don’t we call you Luna instead? That’s much prettier and much easier to say, don’t you think?” A murmur of agreement passed over the crowd. Yet Haewon stayed rooted against the wall, saying nothing.

annotations.comment: Maybe she brings the teacher in on this?

paragraphs.body: “Not even going to thank me for your first American gift, huh?” Sarah asked. She turned to leave, the other girls once more making way. Sarah smiled beautifully over her shoulder. “And here I was, thinking your people were supposed to be polite. Go figure.”

In some ways, Haewon considered her love for dance to be a blessing and a curse. A blessing for granting her opportunities that should have been impossible for the world from which she hailed. A curse for the way her classmates would whisper about her, sometimes with a sly dart of the eye, sometimes with a humorless laugh.

“There goes Miss Too-Good-To-Talk-To-Us,” she’d catch in passing. Or “there goes the snob, the braggart, the bitch.” Haewon didn’t know what all of those words meant, but she could guess well enough. It didn’t help that she was completely inept at matching names to faces. The western names just didn’t stick to her tongue the same way Korean ones did. The mushy English syllables rolled around her mouth, refusing to melt into her tongue the way her mom and dad and sisters’ names did.

annotations.comment: It’s a nice touch that for as much trouble as others have with her name, she has trouble with their names.
annotations.context_left: d-To-Talk-To-Us,” she’d catch in passing. Or “there goes the snob, the braggart, the bitch.” Haewon

paragraphs.body: Ms. Karapandža thought Luna was a lovely name. She praised Sarah for taking the initiative in helping her classmate fit in with the rest. Sarah just smiled prettily, said she was happy to help. Though for the split second her gaze flickered over to Haewon’s, the quiet girl felt a cold knot in the pit of her stomach.

annotations.comment: I wondered if you want to introduce Sarah earlier. It feels like Ms. K is the villain at the beginning of the piece but here, she seems like the dumb adult rube and Sarah the real problem.
annotations.context_left: t in the pit of her

paragraphs.body: It was a few weeks after Haewon arrived at the school that Ms. Karapandža requested her presence after class. As the other girls filed out of the classroom to the cafeteria, Haewon saw that Sarah and two other girls, Jennifer and Claire, were still there as well.

“I’m sure you are wondering why I’ve asked you girls to stay behind,” Ms. Karapandža said, her lips stretching into a thin, taut line. Haewon could never tell when that expression was meant to be wry, or pleased.

“Don’t worry – you’re not in trouble,” the instructor continued. “In fact, I’ve specifically selected you four girls for your merit and outstanding performance in my class thus far.” Jennifer and Claire shared a wide-eyed look, and Sarah seemed to stand up straighter.

“As you know, our fine institution enjoys a close relationship with the New York City Ballet. Many of our own students often transition directly to the City Ballet after they complete their training here at the School of American Ballet.” All four girls perked up at the mention of the City Ballet, even Sarah.

“Their newest creative director, Anders Foqúe, is opening his debut production at the City Ballet this upcoming spring. For his program, ‘Den Lille Prins,’ they have granted us a marvelous opportunity by requesting one girl from our intermediate and advanced programs to be selected for a minor role, the princess’s younger sister. And, seeing as you girls are the top students in my class, I have decided that the four of you will be eligible to audition for the role.” Though she kept her composure, hands folded behind her back, Ms. Karapandža’s eyes gleamed with a pride that would’ve suggested she had directed the new ballet herself. Jennifer and Claire squealed, clapping their hands in delight.

Haewon was too shocked for words. Did I hear her correctly? She wondered. Did she really just say I would have the opportunity to audition for a part in the New York City Ballet? Haewon glanced at Sarah. The girl was breathing heavily, eyes blown wide with the intensity of a ravenous animal. Finally, Sarah cleared her throat and flushed a bright, pearly smile.

annotations.comment: Again, love the plot here. You might think about what would happen if Haewon really doesn’t understand what’s being said to her, or not entirely.
annotations.context_right: for a part in the N

paragraphs.body: “Thank you so much for this special opportunity, Ms. Karapandža,” Sarah said sweetly. “I will work very hard to prepare for the audition.”
“My, what pretty manners, Sarah,” Ms. Karapandža said, rewarding her with a rare smile.
“Yes, thank you, Ms. Karapandža!” The other two girls quickly chimed in.

Ms. Karapandža glanced at Haewon expectantly. Haewon tried to utter the same words, but found herself only capable of gaping stupidly, her mouth bobbing open and closed like a fish. With all eyes on her, she found she couldn’t push a single sound out of her throat.

“Thank you,” she finally stuttered out. Satisfied, the instructor gave a brief nod. “I will have more information regarding the ballet and the audition delivered to your dorms,” she said, turning to the rest. “And up until the audition, you girls will have private lessons with me twice a week in order to prepare. Now, I have kept you from your lunch break long enough – you are dismissed, ladies.” And with that, Ms. Karapandža clapped her hands twice.
By the afternoon, every girl in the advanced division had heard the news. Sarah could not take more than a few steps without being bombarded by girls gushing their congratulations.

Sarah would accept them with an air of grace every time. “Thank you,” she would say, as if Ms. Karapandža had said the part was already hers. Even Jennifer and Claire trailed Sarah wherever she went. They seemed more ecstatic at Sarah’s acknowledgment of them than the prospect of participating in the program themselves.

Contrary to the great attention Sarah received, no one said a word to Haewon all day, let alone congratulated her. But she did not mind – her own wonderment was enough to occupy her every waking thought.

She spent her day imagining what her family would say. How her sisters would flush pink with glee – they wouldn’t quite understand how big an opportunity this was, but they were always proud and affectionate of anything Haewon did. Her Mother and Grandmother might weep old tears, knowing their heartbreak at Haewon’s departure would have been worth it after all. Her father would cross his arms, close his eyes and nod, which Haewon knew meant he was incredibly happy for her.

annotations.comment: This is a good opportunity to make it more clear who she lives with in the U.S.

paragraphs.body: She couldn’t wait to tell them, but she didn’t know when she’d have the chance. The landline telephones greatly confused her, and she disliked asking the Residence Managers for help. They scowled at her broken English, even more so when they realized she was trying to make an expensive international call.
